### [1] [OpenKedge: Governing Agentic Mutation with Execution-Bound Safety and Evidence Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08601)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenKedge is a protocol designed to ensure safety and coordination in autonomous AI systems by redefining state mutation processes, focusing on preventative safeguards and deterministic auditability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fundamental flaws in API-centric architectures that allow probabilistic systems to execute unsupervised state mutations, risking safety and coordination in autonomous AI systems.

Method: OpenKedge employs declarative intent proposals evaluated against system state, policies, and temporal signals before execution, using execution contracts enforced via task-oriented identities and implementing an Intent-to-Execution Evidence Chain for cryptographic auditability.

Result: Evaluations revealed that OpenKedge effectively arbitrates multi-agent conflicts, cages unsafe executions, and maintains high throughput, positioning it as safe and scalable for agentic systems.

Conclusion: OpenKedge provides a reliable framework for secure operation of autonomous systems, emphasizing proactive enforcement and deterministic auditing to ensure safety and coordination at scale.

Abstract: The rise of autonomous AI agents exposes a fundamental flaw in API-centric architectures: probabilistic systems directly execute state mutations without sufficient context, coordination, or safety guarantees. We introduce OpenKedge, a protocol that redefines mutation as a governed process rather than an immediate consequence of API invocation. OpenKedge requires actors to submit declarative intent proposals, which are evaluated against deterministically derived system state, temporal signals, and policy constraints prior to execution. Approved intents are compiled into execution contracts that strictly bound permitted actions, resource scope, and time, and are enforced via ephemeral, task-oriented identities. This shifts safety from reactive filtering to preventative, execution-bound enforcement. Crucially, OpenKedge introduces an Intent-to-Execution Evidence Chain (IEEC), which cryptographically links intent, context, policy decisions, execution bounds, and outcomes into a unified lineage. This transforms mutation into a verifiable and reconstructable process, enabling deterministic auditability and reasoning about system behavior. We evaluate OpenKedge across multi-agent conflict scenarios and cloud infrastructure mutations. Results show that the protocol deterministically arbitrates competing intents and cages unsafe execution while maintaining high throughput, establishing a principled foundation for safely operating agentic systems at scale.

</details>


### [2] [From Business Events to Auditable Decisions: Ontology-Governed Graph Simulation for Enterprise AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08603)
*Hongyin Zhu,Jinming Liang,Mengjun Hou,Ruifan Tang,Xianbin Zhu,Jingyuan Yang,Yuanman Mao,Feng Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces LOM-action, an architecture for enterprise AI that performs event-driven ontology simulation to ground decisions in actionable and audit-traceable context, outperforming existing models significantly in F1 scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based agent systems fail to incorporate the specific context of business events, leading to ungrounded and unauditable decisions.

Method: LOM-action employs a dual-mode architecture where business events trigger updates within a sandboxed enterprise ontology graph, evolving a scenario-valid simulation graph from which decisions are exclusively derived.

Result: LOM-action achieves 93.82% accuracy and a significantly higher F1 score (98.74%) compared to baseline models, which expose issues of deceptive accuracy without high F1 performance.

Conclusion: Ontology-governed, event-driven simulation is critical for building reliable enterprise AI systems, outperforming reliance on model scale alone.

Abstract: Existing LLM-based agent systems share a common architectural failure: they answer from the unrestricted knowledge space without first simulating how active business scenarios reshape that space for the event at hand -- producing decisions that are fluent but ungrounded and carrying no audit trail. We present LOM-action, which equips enterprise AI with \emph{event-driven ontology simulation}: business events trigger scenario conditions encoded in the enterprise ontology~(EO), which drive deterministic graph mutations in an isolated sandbox, evolving a working copy of the subgraph into the scenario-valid simulation graph $G_{\text{sim}}$; all decisions are derived exclusively from this evolved graph. The core pipeline is \emph{event $\to$ simulation $\to$ decision}, realized through a dual-mode architecture -- \emph{skill mode} and \emph{reasoning mode}. Every decision produces a fully traceable audit log. LOM-action achieves 93.82% accuracy and 98.74% tool-chain F1 against frontier baselines Doubao-1.8 and DeepSeek-V3.2, which reach only 24--36% F1 despite 80% accuracy -- exposing the \emph{illusive accuracy} phenomenon. The four-fold F1 advantage confirms that ontology-governed, event-driven simulation, not model scale, is the architectural prerequisite for trustworthy enterprise decision intelligence.

</details>


### [3] [Sustained Impact of Agentic Personalisation in Marketing: A Longitudinal Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08621)
*Olivier Jeunen,Eleanor Hanna,Schaun Wheeler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies a real-world CRM case over 11 months, comparing human-managed versus autonomous messaging strategies. Humans excel in initial engagement lift, but autonomous agents can sustain gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how much human oversight is needed in adaptive learning systems for ensuring CRM performance over time.

Method: The study analyzed 11 months of CRM for a large user base, comparing active human-curated messaging with a passive autonomous system.

Result: Human management achieved higher initial engagement, but autonomous agents maintained positive performance afterward.

Conclusion: A hybrid model is proposed: human intervention for initial strategy and discovery, and autonomous agents for scalable and sustained gains.

Abstract: In consumer applications, Customer Relationship Management (CRM) has traditionally relied on the manual optimisation of static, rule-based messaging strategies. While adaptive and autonomous learning systems offer the promise of scalable personalisation, it remains unclear to what extent ``human-in-the-loop'' oversight is required to sustain performance uplift over time. This paper presents a longitudinal case study analysing a real-world consumer application that leverages agentic infrastructure to personalise marketing messaging for a large-scale user base over an 11-month period.
  We compare two distinct periods: an active phase where marketers directly curated content, audiences, and strategies -- followed immediately by a passive phase where agents operated autonomously from a fixed library of components. Our results demonstrate that whilst active human management generates the highest relative lift in engagement metrics, the autonomous agents successfully sustained a positive lift during the passive period. These findings suggest a symbiotic model where human intervention drives strategic initialisation and discovery, yet autonomous agents can ensure the scalable retention and preservation of performance gains.

</details>


### [4] [RAMP: Hybrid DRL for Online Learning of Numeric Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08685)
*Yarin Benyamin,Argaman Mordoch,Shahaf S. Shperberg,Roni Stern*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RAMP, an approach that combines reinforcement learning, action model learning, and planning to online-learn numeric planning action models, achieving superior performance in numeric planning domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning numeric planning action models is challenging due to existing algorithms being offline and requiring expert-provided traces; the paper aims to enable online learning through interaction with the environment.

Method: The proposed RAMP strategy integrates Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) policy, numeric action model learning, and a planner, creating a positive feedback loop. It also introduces Numeric PDDLGym, a framework for facilitating numeric planning in Gym environments.

Result: RAMP significantly outperforms the PPO algorithm in terms of solvability and plan quality within standard numeric planning domains.

Conclusion: RAMP effectively combines RL, action model learning, and planning, demonstrating its potential to address challenges in numeric domain action model learning and improve planning outcomes.

Abstract: Automated planning algorithms require an action model specifying the preconditions and effects of each action, but obtaining such a model is often hard. Learning action models from observations is feasible, but existing algorithms for numeric domains are offline, requiring expert traces as input. We propose the Reinforcement learning, Action Model learning, and Planning (RAMP) strategy for learning numeric planning action models online via interactions with the environment. RAMP simultaneously trains a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) policy, learns a numeric action model from past interactions, and uses that model to plan future actions when possible. These components form a positive feedback loop: the RL policy gathers data to refine the action model, while the planner generates plans to continue training the RL policy. To facilitate this integration of RL and numeric planning, we developed Numeric PDDLGym, an automated framework for converting numeric planning problems to Gym environments. Experimental results on standard IPC numeric domains show that RAMP significantly outperforms PPO, a well-known DRL algorithm, in terms of solvability and plan quality.

</details>


### [5] [Parameterized Complexity Of Representing Models Of MSO Formulas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08707)
*Petr Kučera,Petr Martinek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper explores extending Courcelle's theorem in MSO2 logic by introducing decision diagrams for models with free variables, proving size bounds based on treewidth and pathwidth, and discussing limitations of OBDDs. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to deepen the utility of Courcelle's theorem by analyzing representation methods for MSO2 formulas using decision diagrams, focusing on computational efficiency.

Method: Utilizing decision diagrams such as sentential decision diagrams (SDD) and ordered binary decision diagrams (OBDD), they examine size parameterizations based on graph treewidth and pathwidth.

Result: They establish parameterized linear upper bounds for SDD and OBDD, and highlight a case where OBDDs fail to achieve this efficiency under bounded treewidth.

Conclusion: The study links Courcelle's theorem to knowledge representation, providing insights into its computational implications and extending its theoretical framework.

Abstract: Monadic second order logic (MSO2) plays an important role in parameterized complexity due to the Courcelle's theorem. This theorem states that the problem of checking if a given graph has a property specified by a given MSO2 formula can be solved by a parameterized linear time algorithm with respect to the treewidth of the graph and the size of the formula. We extend this result by showing that models of MSO2 formula with free variables can be represented with a decision diagram whose size is parameterized linear in the above mentioned parameter. In particular, we show a parameterized linear upper bound on the size of a sentential decision diagram (SDD) when treewidth is considered and a parameterized linear upper bound on the size of an ordered binary decision diagram (OBDD) when considering the pathwidth in the parameter. In addition, building on a lower bound on the size of OBDD by Razgon (2014), we show that there is an MSO2 formula and a class of graphs with bounded treewidth which do not admit an OBDD with the size parameterized by the treewidth. Our result offers a new perspective on the Courcelle's theorem and connects it to the area of knowledge representation.

</details>


### [6] [Model Space Reasoning as Search in Feedback Space for Planning Domain Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08712)
*James Oswald,Daniel Oblinsky,Volodymyr Varha,Vasilije Dragovic,Harsha Kokel,Kavitha Srinivas,Michael Katz,Shirin Sohrabi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores using agentic language model feedback with symbolic information to generate planning domains from natural language descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of generating high-quality planning domains from natural language descriptions, which current large language models struggle to achieve without additional inputs.

Method: The method involves combining symbolic feedback such as landmarks and validator outputs with agentic language model frameworks, and employing heuristic search to optimize the model-space for improving domain quality.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate the role of symbolic feedback in enhancing the quality of planning domains generated by the agentic language model framework.

Conclusion: Integrating symbolic feedback with language model frameworks can significantly improve their ability to produce practical and deployable planning domains from natural language inputs.

Abstract: The generation of planning domains from natural language descriptions remains an open problem even with the advent of large language models and reasoning models. Recent work suggests that while LLMs have the ability to assist with domain generation, they are still far from producing high quality domains that can be deployed in practice. To this end, we investigate the ability of an agentic language model feedback framework to generate planning domains from natural language descriptions that have been augmented with a minimal amount of symbolic information. In particular, we evaluate the quality of the generated domains under various forms of symbolic feedback, including landmarks, and output from the VAL plan validator. Using these feedback mechanisms, we experiment using heuristic search over model space to optimize domain quality.

</details>


### [7] [Artifacts as Memory Beyond the Agent Boundary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08756)
*John D. Martin,Fraser Mince,Esra'a Saleh,Amy Pajak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores how the environment can serve as an agent's memory in Reinforcement Learning, proposing a theoretical framework, backed by experiments showing reduced memory requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research is motivated by the situated cognition theory, which suggests intelligent behavior relies on both internal memory and active environmental resource use.

Method: The authors develop a mathematical model showing how environmental observations can act as memory, introducing the concept of 'artifacts' to reduce required historical information.

Result: Experiments demonstrated reduced memory requirements for agents observing spatial paths, with the effect arising naturally through the sensory stream.

Conclusion: The environment can be utilized as external memory in RL, suggesting future research to explore principled methods for leveraging it as a substitute for internal memory.

Abstract: The situated view of cognition holds that intelligent behavior depends not only on internal memory, but on an agent's active use of environmental resources. Here, we begin formalizing this intuition within Reinforcement Learning (RL). We introduce a mathematical framing for how the environment can functionally serve as an agent's memory, and prove that certain observations, which we call artifacts, can reduce the information needed to represent history. We corroborate our theory with experiments showing that when agents observe spatial paths, the amount of memory required to learn a performant policy is reduced. Interestingly, this effect arises unintentionally, and implicitly through the agent's sensory stream. We discuss the implications of our findings, and show they satisfy qualitative properties previously used to ground accounts of external memory. Moving forward, we anticipate further work on this subject could reveal principled ways to exploit the environment as a substitute for explicit internal memory.

</details>


### [8] [Hidden in Plain Sight: Visual-to-Symbolic Analytical Solution Inference from Field Visualizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08863)
*Pengze Li,Jiaquan Zhang,Yunbo Long,Xinping Liu,Zhou wenjie,Encheng Su,Zihang Zeng,Jiaqi Liu,Jiyao Liu,Junchi Yu,Lihao Liu,Philip Torr,Shixiang Tang,Aoran Wang,Xi Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a pipeline (ViSA-R2) to infer and verify analytical solutions of steady-state fields from visual data with minimal metadata. It sets benchmarks (ViSA-Bench) and shows better performance compared to existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of deriving analytical solutions of physical fields from visual data to advance AI-assisted scientific reasoning.

Method: Develop a self-verifying pipeline (ViSA-R2) based on structural pattern recognition, solution-family hypothesis, parameter derivation, and consistency verification. Release ViSA-Bench benchmark for comprehensive evaluation.

Result: ViSA-R2 demonstrates superior performance over strong open-source baselines and tested closed-source VLMs, evaluated based on numerical accuracy, expression similarity, and character-level metrics.

Conclusion: ViSA-R2 shows promise in automating symbolic solution derivation, indicating potential scalability and applications in physics-related AI innovations.

Abstract: Recovering analytical solutions of physical fields from visual observations is a fundamental yet underexplored capability for AI-assisted scientific reasoning. We study visual-to-symbolic analytical solution inference (ViSA) for two-dimensional linear steady-state fields: given field visualizations (and first-order derivatives) plus minimal auxiliary metadata, the model must output a single executable SymPy expression with fully instantiated numeric constants. We introduce ViSA-R2 and align it with a self-verifying, solution-centric chain-of-thought pipeline that follows a physicist-like pathway: structural pattern recognition solution-family (ansatz) hypothesis parameter derivation consistency verification. We also release ViSA-Bench, a VLM-ready synthetic benchmark covering 30 linear steady-state scenarios with verifiable analytical/symbolic annotations, and evaluate predictions by numerical accuracy, expression-structure similarity, and character-level accuracy. Using an 8B open-weight Qwen3-VL backbone, ViSA-R2 outperforms strong open-source baselines and the evaluated closed-source frontier VLMs under a standardized protocol.

</details>


### [9] [SPPO: Sequence-Level PPO for Long-Horizon Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08865)
*Tianyi Wang,Yixia Li,Long Li,Yibiao Chen,Shaohan Huang,Yun Chen,Peng Li,Yang Liu,Guanhua Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Sequence-Level PPO (SPPO), a scalable framework that improves the alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) for reasoning tasks by offering better stability and resource efficiency compared to existing methods like PPO and GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address limitations in standard PPO, such as instability in reasoning tasks with long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) horizons and high memory costs, as well as shortcomings of critic-free alternatives like GRPO, which have high computational overhead.

Method: The SPPO algorithm redefines reasoning as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit problem and employs a decoupled scalar value function to provide low-variance advantage signals without requiring multi-sampling.

Result: Experiments on mathematical benchmarks show that SPPO greatly improves performance over standard PPO and matches the efficacy of computation-intensive group-based methods.

Conclusion: SPPO offers a resource-efficient and stable framework for aligning reasoning Large Language Models, making it a practical alternative to existing methodologies.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is central to aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) in reasoning tasks with verifiable rewards. However, standard token-level PPO struggles in this setting due to the instability of temporal credit assignment over long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) horizons and the prohibitive memory cost of the value model. While critic-free alternatives like GRPO mitigate these issues, they incur significant computational overhead by requiring multiple samples for baseline estimation, severely limiting training throughput. In this paper, we introduce Sequence-Level PPO (SPPO), a scalable algorithm that harmonizes the sample efficiency of PPO with the stability of outcome-based updates. SPPO reformulates the reasoning process as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit problem, employing a decoupled scalar value function to derive low-variance advantage signals without multi-sampling. Extensive experiments on mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that SPPO significantly surpasses standard PPO and matches the performance of computation-heavy group-based methods, offering a resource-efficient framework for aligning reasoning LLMs.

</details>


### [10] [StaRPO: Stability-Augmented Reinforcement Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08905)
*Jinghan Zhang,Fengran Mo,Tharindu Cyril Weerasooriya,Ruimin Dai,Xiaoyan Han,Yanjie Fu,Dakuo Wang,Kunpeng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces StaRPO, a reinforcement learning framework that improves reasoning accuracy and logical stability in large language models by explicitly incorporating reasoning stability into the optimization process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the logical inconsistency, structural irregularity, and redundancy in reasoning processes of existing RL policy optimization frameworks which focus only on final-answer correctness.

Method: StaRPO integrates reasoning stability into RL objectives using two lightweight metrics: Autocorrelation Function (ACF) for local coherence and Path Efficiency (PE) for global goal-directedness. These metrics provide process-aware rewards, combined with task-specific rewards.

Result: The framework improves final-answer accuracy and logical stability, as validated on two backbone models and four reasoning benchmarks where StaRPO consistently outperforms baseline approaches.

Conclusion: StaRPO enhances the reasoning efficacy of large language models by delivering more coherent, goal-directed, and accurate logical reasoning through stability-aware reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is effective in enhancing the accuracy of large language models in complex reasoning tasks. Existing RL policy optimization frameworks rely on final-answer correctness as feedback signals and rarely capture the internal logical structure of the reasoning process. Consequently, the models would generate fluent and semantically relevant responses but logically inconsistent, structurally erratic, or redundant. To this end, we propose StaRPO, a stability-augmented reinforcement learning framework that explicitly incorporates reasoning stability into the optimization objective. Our StaRPO decomposes stability into two computable lightweight metrics: the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) to evaluate local step-to-step coherence, and Path Efficiency (PE) to evaluate global goal-directedness of the reasoning trajectory. These stability rewards are combined with task rewards to provide complementary and process-aware feedback. We validate the effectiveness of using ACF and PE rewards by showing their correlation with logic errors on two backbone models. Experiments on four reasoning benchmarks show that StaRPO consistently outperforms compared baselines and can enhance both final-answer accuracy and logical stability.

</details>


### [11] [Enhancing LLM Problem Solving via Tutor-Student Multi-Agent Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08931)
*Nurullah Eymen Özdemir,Erhan Oztop*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a tutor-student multi-agent system (PETITE) to enhance Large Language Model (LLM) performance through role-differentiated interactions on coding tasks, achieving competitive accuracy with fewer resources compared to state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to explore whether structured social interaction inspired by human cognitive development can improve LLM's problem-solving capabilities.

Method: The introduced PETITE framework assigns asymmetric roles to two agents derived from the same LLM: a student agent generates and refines solutions, and a tutor agent provides evaluative feedback with no access to ground-truth answers.

Result: PETITE achieves similar or better accuracy on the APPS coding benchmark compared to state-of-the-art methods, while significantly reducing token consumption.

Conclusion: Developmentally inspired structured interactions, like tutor-student roles, provide an efficient and effective strategy for enhancing LLM problem-solving capabilities.

Abstract: Human cognitive development is shaped not only by individual effort but by structured social interaction, where role-based exchanges such as those between a tutor and a learner, enable solutions that neither could achieve alone. Inspired by these developmental principles, we ask the question whether a tutor-student multi-agent system can create a synergistic effect by pushing Large Language Model (LLM) beyond what it can do within existing frameworks. To test the idea, we adopt autonomous coding problem domain where two agents instantiated from the same LLM assigned asymmetric roles: a student agent generates and iteratively refines solutions, while a tutor agent provides structured evaluative feedback without access to ground-truth answers. In our proposed framework (PETITE), we aim to extract better problem-solving performance from one model by structuring its interaction through complementary roles, rather than relying on stronger supervisory models or heterogeneous ensembles. Our model is evaluated on the APPS coding benchmark against state-of-the-art approaches of Self-Consistency, Self-Refine, Multi-Agent Debate, and Multi-Agent Review. The results show that our model achieves similar or higher accuracy while consuming significantly fewer tokens. These results suggest that developmentally grounded role-differentiated interaction structures provide a principled and resource-efficient paradigm for enhancing LLM problem-solving through structured peer-like interactions. Index Terms- Peer Tutoring, Scaffolding, Large Language Models, Multi-Agent Systems, Code Generation

</details>


### [12] [PilotBench: A Benchmark for General Aviation Agents with Safety Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08987)
*Yalun Wu,Haotian Liu,Zhoujun Li,Boyang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PilotBench evaluates large language models (LLMs) on safety-critical flight prediction tasks using real-world aviation data, revealing key strengths and limitations in their reasoning and precision capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to evaluate whether LLMs are capable of reasoning about complex, safety-critical physical environments, bridging the gap between semantic understanding and physics-governed predictions.

Method: PilotBench is introduced as a benchmark using 708 general aviation trajectories and synchronized telemetry data, alongside 'Pilot-Score,' a composite metric assessing regression accuracy and instruction adherence. The study compares the performance of 41 LLMs with traditional forecasters.

Result: Traditional forecasters excel in precision (MAE of 7.01) but lack semantic reasoning, while LLMs have strong instruction adherence (86-89%) with reduced precision (MAE 11-14). A phase-stratified study uncovers performance challenges for LLMs in high-complexity flight phases.

Conclusion: The findings reveal a trade-off between LLMs' semantic controllability and traditional forecasters' precision, suggesting that hybrid systems integrating both approaches could address these limitations and advance embodied AI.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) advance toward embodied AI agents operating in physical environments, a fundamental question emerges: can models trained on text corpora reliably reason about complex physics while adhering to safety constraints? We address this through PilotBench, a benchmark evaluating LLMs on safety-critical flight trajectory and attitude prediction. Built from 708 real-world general aviation trajectories spanning nine operationally distinct flight phases with synchronized 34-channel telemetry, PilotBench systematically probes the intersection of semantic understanding and physics-governed prediction through comparative analysis of LLMs and traditional forecasters. We introduce Pilot-Score, a composite metric balancing 60% regression accuracy with 40% instruction adherence and safety compliance. Comparative evaluation across 41 models uncovers a Precision-Controllability Dichotomy: traditional forecasters achieve superior MAE of 7.01 but lack semantic reasoning capabilities, while LLMs gain controllability with 86--89% instruction-following at the cost of 11--14 MAE precision. Phase-stratified analysis further exposes a Dynamic Complexity Gap-LLM performance degrades sharply in high-workload phases such as Climb and Approach, suggesting brittle implicit physics models. These empirical discoveries motivate hybrid architectures combining LLMs' symbolic reasoning with specialized forecasters' numerical precision. PilotBench provides a rigorous foundation for advancing embodied AI in safety-constrained domains.

</details>


### [13] [SEA-Eval: A Benchmark for Evaluating Self-Evolving Agents Beyond Episodic Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08988)
*Sihang Jiang,Lipeng Ma,Zhonghua Hong,Keyi Wang,Zhiyu Lu,Shisong Chen,Jinghao Zhang,Tianjun Pan,Weijia Zhou,Jiaqing Liang,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SEA-Eval, a benchmark evaluating long-term evolutionary and intra-task reliability of Self-Evolving Agents (SEA), revealing significant gaps in existing frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based agents excel in task execution but lack the ability to accumulate experience and evolve strategies beyond single tasks, necessitating a framework for evaluating these traits.

Method: The authors propose SEA, a new formal definition for self-evolving agents, and SEA-Eval, a benchmark that uses sequential task streams to measure Success Rate and Token Consumption as proxies for evolution and reliability.

Result: Empirical results show significant evolutionary limitations in state-of-the-art agents, with identical success rates obscuring large disparities in efficiency (up to 31.2x difference in token consumption) and evolutionary progress.

Conclusion: SEA-Eval establishes a robust framework for analyzing and driving the evolution of digital agents toward self-improvement and long-term adaptability, a step beyond static episodic performance.

Abstract: Current LLM-based agents demonstrate strong performance in episodic task execution but remain constrained by static toolsets and episodic amnesia, failing to accumulate experience or optimize strategies across task boundaries. While the Self-Evolving Agent (SEA) paradigm has been previously proposed, this paper contributes a new formal definition of SEA grounded in digital embodiment and continuous cross-task evolution, and introduces SEA-Eval, the first benchmark designed to evaluate SEA characteristics across two dimensions, intra-task execution reliability and long-term evolutionary performance. By organizing tasks into sequential streams and analyzing Success Rate and Token Consumption over time, SEA-Eval quantifies evolutionary gain and structural stability in ways that existing episodic benchmarks cannot. Empirical evaluations reveal a significant evolutionary bottleneck in current state-of-the-art frameworks, where identical success rates mask up to 31.2 times differences in token consumption and divergent evolutionary trajectories under sequential analysis. SEA-Eval provides a rigorous scientific foundation for advancing agents from mere task executors toward genuinely self-evolving digital entities.

</details>


### [14] [Hypergraph Neural Networks Accelerate MUS Enumeration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09001)
*Hiroya Ijima,Koichiro Yawata*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a domain-agnostic method using Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNNs) to accelerate the enumeration of Minimal Unsatisfiable Subsets (MUSes) in constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MUS enumeration in CSPs faces challenges due to the exponential growth of the search space, worsened by expensive satisfiability checks. Existing machine learning techniques address this issue for Boolean problems but are limited by the need for explicit variable-constraint relationships.

Method: The authors propose using HGNNs trained via reinforcement learning to incrementally construct a hypergraph. Constraints act as vertices, MUSes as hyperedges, optimizing satisfiability checks during MUS enumeration.

Result: Experimental results show that the proposed HGNN-based method enumerates more MUSes within the same satisfiability check budget compared to traditional approaches.

Conclusion: The approach effectively accelerates MUS enumeration, demonstrating the advantages of applying HGNNs in domain-agnostic scenarios for CSPs.

Abstract: Enumerating Minimal Unsatisfiable Subsets (MUSes) is a fundamental task in constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs). Its major challenge is the exponential growth of the search space, which becomes particularly severe when satisfiability checks are expensive. Recent machine learning approaches reduce this cost for Boolean satisfiability problems but rely on explicit variable-constraint relationships, limiting their application domains. This paper proposes a domain-agnostic method to accelerate MUS enumeration using Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNNs). The proposed method incrementally builds a hypergraph with constraints as vertices and MUSes enumerated until the current step as hyperedges, and employs an HGNN-based agent trained via reinforcement learning to minimize the number of satisfiability checks required to obtain an MUS. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in accelerating MUS enumeration, showing that our method can enumerate more MUSes within the same satisfiability check budget compared to conventional methods.

</details>


### [15] [Advantage-Guided Diffusion for Model-Based Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09035)
*Daniele Foffano,Arvid Eriksson,David Broman,Karl H. Johansson,Alexandre Proutiere*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Advantage-Guided Diffusion for Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (AGD-MBRL) to overcome short-horizon myopia in diffusion world models by using advantage estimates to guide trajectory sampling, significantly improving sample efficiency and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While diffusion world models mitigate compounding errors inherent in autoregressive methods for MBRL, existing diffusion guides often lack long-term reward focus, either being purely policy-based or short-term reward-driven. This results in limited effectiveness in optimizing long-term returns.

Method: The authors propose AGD-MBRL, which employs advantage estimates to steer the reverse diffusion process toward trajectories with higher long-term return. They introduce Sigmoid and Exponential Advantage Guidance to reweight trajectory sampling and achieve policy improvement. AGD integrates smoothly with PolyGRAD architectures without altering the training objective.

Result: On MuJoCo control benchmarks like HalfCheetah and Walker2D, AGD-MBRL demonstrates significant gains in sample efficiency and final returns compared to PolyGRAD, Diffuser-style reward guides, and model-free approaches (PPO/TRPO), outperforming some methods by up to 2x.

Conclusion: Advantage-aware diffusion modeling (AGD-MBRL) effectively addresses short-horizon myopia, offering a simple yet impactful improvement in reinforcement learning by leveraging trajectory advantage information for better long-term outcomes.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) with autoregressive world models suffers from compounding errors, whereas diffusion world models mitigate this by generating trajectory segments jointly. However, existing diffusion guides are either policy-only, discarding value information, or reward-based, which becomes myopic when the diffusion horizon is short. We introduce Advantage-Guided Diffusion for MBRL (AGD-MBRL), which steers the reverse diffusion process using the agent's advantage estimates so that sampling concentrates on trajectories expected to yield higher long-term return beyond the generated window. We develop two guides: (i) Sigmoid Advantage Guidance (SAG) and (ii) Exponential Advantage Guidance (EAG). We prove that a diffusion model guided through SAG or EAG allows us to perform reweighted sampling of trajectories with weights increasing in state-action advantage-implying policy improvement under standard assumptions. Additionally, we show that the trajectories generated from AGD-MBRL follow an improved policy (that is, with higher value) compared to an unguided diffusion model. AGD integrates seamlessly with PolyGRAD-style architectures by guiding the state components while leaving action generation policy-conditioned, and requires no change to the diffusion training objective. On MuJoCo control tasks (HalfCheetah, Hopper, Walker2D and Reacher), AGD-MBRL improves sample efficiency and final return over PolyGRAD, an online Diffuser-style reward guide, and model-free baselines (PPO/TRPO), in some cases by a margin of 2x. These results show that advantage-aware guidance is a simple, effective remedy for short-horizon myopia in diffusion-model MBRL.

</details>


### [16] [Overhang Tower: Resource-Rational Adaptation in Sequential Physical Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09072)
*Ruihong Shen,Shiqian Li,Yixin Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper explores how humans adapt physical prediction mechanisms and planning strategies under cognitive resource limitations, unifying debates on simulation vs. heuristics and myopic vs. deliberative planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how humans integrate physical prediction and planning strategies under resource constraints, addressing gaps in the debates on intuitive prediction models and decision-making strategies.

Method: The authors used a task called Overhang Tower, where participants constructed structures requiring both physical stability and planning. They analyzed shifts in human prediction and planning mechanisms under varying cognitive pressures, such as complexity and time.

Result: Humans exhibit a dual transition under resource pressure: they shift from Intuitive Physics Engine (IPE)-based simulation to CNN-based heuristics as task complexity grows and from deliberative planning to shallower lookahead strategies under time pressure.

Conclusion: Humans possess a resource-rational architecture, dynamically trading off computational costs for predictive fidelity, thereby unifying debates on physical prediction and planning strategies into a cohesive framework.

Abstract: Humans effortlessly navigate the physical world by predicting how objects behave under gravity and contact forces, yet how such judgments support sequential physical planning under resource constraints remains poorly understood. Research on intuitive physics debates whether prediction relies on the Intuitive Physics Engine (IPE) or fast, cue-based heuristics; separately, decision-making research debates deliberative lookahead versus myopic strategies. These debates have proceeded in isolation, leaving the cognitive architecture of sequential physical planning underspecified. How physical prediction mechanisms and planning strategies jointly adapt under limited cognitive resources remains an open question. Here we show that humans exhibit a dual transition under resource pressure, simultaneously shifting both physical prediction mechanism and planning strategy to match cognitive budget. Using Overhang Tower, a construction task requiring participants to maximize horizontal overhang while maintaining stability, we find that IPE-based simulation dominates early stages while CNN-based visual heuristics prevail as complexity grows; concurrently, time pressure truncates deliberative lookahead, shifting planning toward shallower horizons: a dual transition unpredicted by prior single-mechanism accounts. These findings reveal a hierarchical, resource-rational architecture that flexibly trades computational cost against predictive fidelity. Our results unify two long-standing debates (simulation vs. heuristics and myopic vs. deliberative planning) as a dynamic repertoire reconfigured by cognitive budget.

</details>


### [17] [Camera Artist: A Multi-Agent Framework for Cinematic Language Storytelling Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09195)
*Haobo Hu,Qi Mao,Yuanhang Li,Libiao Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Camera Artist introduces a novel multi-agent framework for generating narrative videos with enhanced cinematic quality, addressing issues in narrative continuity and expressiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent systems for filmmaking struggle with maintaining narrative progression and cinematic language, leading to fragmented storytelling and suboptimal filmic quality.

Method: The framework incorporates a Cinematography Shot Agent for enhancing narrative continuity via recursive storyboard generation and injects cinematic language for expressive shot designs.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show Camera Artist consistently surpasses baseline approaches in narrative consistency, dynamic expressiveness, and overall film quality.

Conclusion: Camera Artist significantly improves video storytelling with structured narrative progression and refined cinematic aesthetics, advancing automated filmmaking systems.

Abstract: We propose Camera Artist, a multi-agent framework that models a real-world filmmaking workflow to generate narrative videos with explicit cinematic language. While recent multi-agent systems have made substantial progress in automating filmmaking workflows from scripts to videos, they often lack explicit mechanisms to structure narrative progression across adjacent shots and deliberate use of cinematic language, resulting in fragmented storytelling and limited filmic quality. To address this, Camera Artist builds upon established agentic pipelines and introduces a dedicated Cinematography Shot Agent, which integrates recursive storyboard generation to strengthen shot-to-shot narrative continuity and cinematic language injection to produce more expressive, film-oriented shot designs. Extensive quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines in narrative consistency, dynamic expressiveness, and perceived film quality.

</details>


### [18] [DRBENCHER: Can Your Agent Identify the Entity, Retrieve Its Properties and Do the Math?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09251)
*Young-Suk Lee,Ramon Fernandez Astudillo,Radu Florian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DRBENCHER is a synthetic benchmark generator designed to evaluate agent capabilities requiring both web browsing and multi-step computation. It spans multiple domains and emphasizes verifiable, complex, diverse, and difficult question generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world performance evaluation of agents is limited due to isolated testing of web browsing and computation. This paper aims to bridge that gap by developing a benchmark integrating both capabilities.

Method: DRBENCHER creates synthetic questions based on a unified answer-first pipeline with criteria like verifiability, complexity, difficulty, and diversity. It spans five domains and incorporates human and automated evaluations.

Result: Human evaluation confirmed 76% validity with 35% of errors arising from outdated knowledge graphs; automatic evaluation showed only 20% accuracy by the strongest model. DRBENCHER demonstrated superior semantic diversity.

Conclusion: The benchmark addresses limitations in assessing real-world agent capabilities, setting a high standard for multi-modal agents and emphasizing challenges with evolving data sources.

Abstract: Deep research agents increasingly interleave web browsing with multi-step computation, yet existing benchmarks evaluate these capabilities in isolation, creating a blind spot in assessing real-world performance. We introduce DRBENCHER, a synthetic benchmark generator for questions that require both browsing and computation. It enforces four criteria: verifiability (gold answers are computed by executing parameterized code over knowledge-graph values), complexity (multi-hop entity identification, property retrieval, and domain-specific computation), difficulty (a two-stage verification cascade filters out questions solvable by the generating model), and diversity (a greedy max-min embedding filter maximizes coverage). These criteria are realized via a unified answer-first pipeline spanning five domains: biochemistry, financial, geophysical, security, and history. Human evaluation shows 76% validity (84% excluding stale data), with 35% of errors due to outdated knowledge-graph entries, highlighting an inherent limitation of systems that reason over evolving data. Automatic evaluation shows that the strongest frontier model achieves only 20% answer accuracy. Compared to manually constructed benchmarks (BrowseComp+, MATH-500, GPQA), DRBENCHER achieves the highest semantic diversity.

</details>


### [19] [SAGE: A Service Agent Graph-guided Evaluation Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09285)
*Ling Shi,Yuqin Dai,Ziyin Wang,Ning Gao,Wei Zhang,Chaozheng Wang,Yujie Wang,Wei He,Jinpeng Wang,Deiyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SAGE, a benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) in customer service scenarios, addressing gaps in assessing logical compliance and user interaction diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks do not fully capture diverse user behaviors or align with the structured adherence to SOPs required in real-world customer service deployments.

Method: SAGE uses Dynamic Dialogue Graphs to formalize SOPs, integrates an Adversarial Intent Taxonomy and Extension Mechanism for multi-domain deployment, and employs a judge-based evaluation framework with a Rule Engine for interaction analysis.

Result: SAGE tests on 27 LLMs in 6 industrial cases found significant gaps in execution (correct intent recognition but poor action derivation) and observed LLMs' "Empathy Resilience" under adversarial stress.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark provides a comprehensive, dual-axis evaluation solution, highlighting critical issues like execution gaps in LLMs while ensuring deployment adaptability across different domains.

Abstract: The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has catalyzed automation in customer service, yet benchmarking their performance remains challenging. Existing benchmarks predominantly rely on static paradigms and single-dimensional metrics, failing to account for diverse user behaviors or the strict adherence to structured Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) required in real-world deployments. To bridge this gap, we propose SAGE (Service Agent Graph-guided Evaluation), a universal multi-agent benchmark for automated, dual-axis assessment. SAGE formalizes unstructured SOPs into Dynamic Dialogue Graphs, enabling precise verification of logical compliance and comprehensive path coverage. We introduce an Adversarial Intent Taxonomy and a modular Extension Mechanism, enabling low-cost deployment across domains and facilitating automated dialogue data synthesis. Evaluation is conducted via a framework where Judge Agents and a Rule Engine analyze interactions between User and Service Agents to generate deterministic ground truth. Extensive experiments on 27 LLMs across 6 industrial scenarios reveal a significant ``Execution Gap'' where models accurately classify intents but fail to derive correct subsequent actions. We also observe ``Empathy Resilience'', a phenomenon where models maintain polite conversational facades despite underlying logical failures under high adversarial intensity. Code and resources are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SAGE-Bench-4CD3/.

</details>


### [20] [Constraint-Aware Corrective Memory for Language-Based Drug Discovery Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09308)
*Maochen Sun,Youzhi Zhang,Gaofeng Meng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CACM, a framework for improving language-based drug discovery by incorporating set-level diagnostics and efficient memory management to address protocol-level requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing drug discovery systems struggle because they depend on long, noisy histories and poorly-defined self-reflection, making it difficult to localize failures and maintain effective decision-making.

Method: CACM uses protocol auditing and a diagnostician to analyze multimodal evidence for identifying protocol violations and generating correction hints. It organizes memory into static, dynamic, and corrective channels to compress planning context and preserve relevant information.

Result: CACM improved the target success rate by 36.4% compared to the state-of-the-art, demonstrating better reliability in language-driven drug discovery.

Conclusion: Language-based drug discovery can significantly benefit from enhanced diagnostic precision and efficient memory, not just powerful molecular tools.

Abstract: Large language models are making autonomous drug discovery agents increasingly feasible, but reliable success in this setting is not determined by any single action or molecule. It is determined by whether the final returned set jointly satisfies protocol-level requirements such as set size, diversity, binding quality, and developability. This creates a fundamental control problem: the agent plans step by step, while task validity is decided at the level of the whole candidate set. Existing language-based drug discovery systems therefore tend to rely on long raw history and under-specified self-reflection, making failure localization imprecise and planner-facing agent states increasingly noisy. We present CACM (Constraint-Aware Corrective Memory), a language-based drug discovery framework built around precise set-level diagnosis and a concise memory write-back mechanism. CACM introduces protocol auditing and a grounded diagnostician, which jointly analyze multimodal evidence spanning task requirements, pocket context, and candidate-set evidence to localize protocol violations, generate actionable remediation hints, and bias the next action toward the most relevant correction. To keep planning context compact, CACM organizes memory into static, dynamic, and corrective channels and compresses them before write-back, thereby preserving persistent task information while exposing only the most decision-relevant failures. Our experimental results show that CACM improves the target-level success rate by 36.4% over the state-of-the-art baseline. The results show that reliable language-based drug discovery benefits not only from more powerful molecular tools, but also from more precise diagnosis and more economical agent states.

</details>


### [21] [Mind the Gap Between Spatial Reasoning and Acting! Step-by-Step Evaluation of Agents With Spatial-Gym](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09338)
*Lars Benedikt Kaesberg,Tianyu Yang,Niklas Bauer,Terry Ruas,Jan Philip Wahle,Bela Gipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Spatial-Gym, a testing framework for spatial reasoning in robotic and navigation tasks, revealing significant accuracy gaps between current models and humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial reasoning is a critical component for navigation and robotics, but existing benchmarks aren't interactive like human decision-making processes.

Method: They developed Spatial-Gym, a 2D-grid puzzle environment for evaluating spatial constraint reasoning as a sequential decision task, and tested multiple models with various settings against human and algorithmic baselines.

Result: The best model achieved a 16.0% solve rate, significantly below human performance (98%). Key findings include performance breakdown with increasing difficulty, and chain-of-thought reasoning outperforming standard inference.

Conclusion: Spatial-Gym serves as a valuable tool for identifying limitations in model-based spatial reasoning and points towards directions for improvement, including reinforcement learning adaptations.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning is central to navigation and robotics, yet measuring model capabilities on these tasks remains difficult. Existing benchmarks evaluate models in a one-shot setting, requiring full solution generation in a single response, unlike humans, who work in interactive environments step-by-step. We introduce Spatial-Gym, a Gymnasium environment that isolates spatial constraint reasoning by testing pathfinding in 2D-grid puzzles as a sequential decision task with optional backtracking. We evaluate eight models in three settings (one-shot, step-by-step, step-by-step with backtracking) against human, random, and A* baselines on 500 episodes. The best model, GPT-OSS 120B, achieves a solve rate of 16.0%, 82 points below the human baseline (98.0%). Step-by-step format helps weaker models (up to +5.4%) by removing formatting errors, but hurts stronger models (up to 5.6%) by constraining global planning. Backtracking improves episode completion, but increases solve rate only for weaker models; stronger models rarely backtrack and do not benefit from it. Our experiments have three key findings: (1) models fail to scale reasoning effort with difficulty, (2) vision models receiving images of the spatial environment reduce solve rate by 73%, and (3) extended chain-of-thought reasoning retains a 3-5x accuracy advantage over standard inference even in the step-by-step setting. Spatial-Gym enables diagnosis of model limitations and provides a framework for improving spatial reasoning through reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [22] [HiL-Bench (Human-in-Loop Benchmark): Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09408)
*Mohamed Elfeki,Tu Trinh,Kelvin Luu,Guangze Luo,Nathan Hunt,Ernesto Montoya,Nandan Marwaha,Yannis He,Charles Wang,Fernando Crabedo,Alessa Castilo,Bing Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper addresses the judgment issue in AI models, focusing on their failure to selectively seek help when faced with incomplete or ambiguous task specifications. The proposed HiL-Bench evaluates the ability to escalate appropriately by incorporating human-validated blockers and introduces the Ask-F1 metric to balance over-asking and silent guessing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the gap in AI model evaluation, where current benchmarks ignore the skill of knowing when to seek help, leading models to fail when faced with incomplete or ambiguous task requirements.

Method: The authors introduce the HiL-Bench, a benchmark designed to measure selective escalation skills in AI models. It includes tasks with built-in blockers and evaluates models using the Ask-F1 metric, which balances question precision with blocker recall.

Result: The study shows that frontier models significantly underperform in help-seeking compared to their full-information task performance. It identifies three failure modes: overconfidence without detecting gaps, recognizing uncertainty but persisting in errors, and overly broad escalation strategies.

Conclusion: The findings reveal that poor help-seeking is a fundamental flaw in current AI models. However, with reinforcement learning (RL) on the Ask-F1 reward, these skills are trainable and transferable across domains. Models can learn to detect unresolvable uncertainties and act accordingly without relying on domain-specific heuristics.

Abstract: Frontier coding agents solve complex tasks when given complete context but collapse when specifications are incomplete or ambiguous. The bottleneck is not raw capability, but judgment: knowing when to act autonomously and when to ask for help. Current benchmarks are blind to this failure mode. They supply unambiguous detailed instructions and solely reward execution correctness, so an agent that makes a lucky guess for a missing requirement will score identically to one that would have asked to be certain.
  We present HiL-Bench (Human-in-the-Loop Benchmark) to measure this selective escalation skill. Each task contains human-validated blockers (missing information, ambiguous requests, contradictory information) that surface only through progressive exploration, not upfront inspection. Our core metric, Ask-F1, the harmonic mean of question precision and blocker recall, captures the tension between over-asking and silent guessing; its structure architecturally prevents gaming through question spam.
  Evaluation across SWE and text-to-SQL domains reveals a large universal judgment gap: no frontier model recovers more than a fraction of its full-information performance when deciding whether to ask. Failure analysis identifies three key help-seeking patterns: overconfident wrong beliefs with no gap detection; high uncertainty detection yet persistent errors; broad, imprecise escalation without self-correction. These consistent patterns confirm poor help-seeking is a model-level flaw, not task-specific. RL training on shaped Ask-F1 reward shows judgment is trainable: a 32B model improves both help-seeking quality and task pass rate, with gains that transfer across domains. The model does not learn domain-specific heuristics for when to ask; it learns to detect unresolvable uncertainty and act on it.

</details>


### [23] [Do We Really Need to Approach the Entire Pareto Front in Many-Objective Bayesian Optimisation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09417)
*Chao Jiang,Jingyu Huang,Miqing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Single Point-based Multi-Objective (SPMO) search framework to address challenges in many-objective Bayesian optimisation, focusing on finding one optimal solution rather than approximating the entire Pareto front.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiencies in many-objective Bayesian optimisation where representing the entire Pareto front is computationally infeasible under limited evaluation budgets.

Method: Introduces SPMO framework and an acquisition function, Expected Single-Point Improvement (ESPI), which is optimised using gradient-based methods through the Sample Average Approximation (SAA) approach.

Result: SPMO is computationally efficient, offers convergence guarantees under SAA, and outperforms existing methods on benchmark and real-world problems.

Conclusion: Focusing on a single, high-quality solution is more practical under evaluation constraints, and SPMO provides an effective and theoretical sound framework for this purpose.

Abstract: Many-objective optimisation, a subset of multi-objective optimisation, involves optimisation problems with more than three objectives. As the number of objectives increases, the number of solutions needed to adequately represent the entire Pareto front typically grows substantially. This makes it challenging, if not infeasible, to design a search algorithm capable of effectively exploring the entire Pareto front. This difficulty is particularly acute in the Bayesian optimisation paradigm, where sample efficiency is critical and only a limited number of solutions (often a few hundred) are evaluated. Moreover, after the optimisation process, the decision-maker eventually selects just one solution for deployment, regardless of how many high-quality, diverse solutions are available. In light of this, we argue an idea that under a very limited evaluation budget, it may be more useful to focus on finding a single solution of the highest possible quality for the decision-maker, rather than aiming to approximate the entire Pareto front as existing many-/multi-objective Bayesian optimisation methods typically do. Bearing this idea in mind, this paper proposes a \underline{s}ingle \underline{p}oint-based \underline{m}ulti-\underline{o}bjective search framework (SPMO) that aims to improve the quality of solutions along a direction that leads to a good tradeoff between objectives. Within SPMO, we present a simple acquisition function, called expected single-point improvement (ESPI), working under both noiseless and noisy scenarios. We show that ESPI can be optimised effectively with gradient-based methods via the sample average approximation (SAA) approach and theoretically prove its convergence guarantees under the SAA. We also empirically demonstrate that the proposed SPMO is computationally tractable and outperforms state-of-the-arts on a wide range of benchmark and real-world problems.

</details>


### [24] [E3-TIR: Enhanced Experience Exploitation for Tool-Integrated Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09455)
*Weiyang Guo,Zesheng Shi,Liye Zhao,Jiayuan Ma,Zeen Zhu,Junxian He,Min Zhang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces E3-TIR, an innovative training paradigm addressing efficiency issues in tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) by dynamically balancing expert and self-exploration strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training paradigms for tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) using large language models (LLMs) face challenges like inefficient exploration in Zero-RL and high data costs or performance plateaus in SFT-then-RL.

Method: The proposed E3-TIR dynamically integrates three experience types: Expert Prefixes, Expert Guided, and Self-Exploration, balancing exploration diversity and efficiency with a mix-policy optimization mechanism.

Result: Experimental evaluations show a 6 improvement in performance compared to traditional paradigms, reduced synthetic data usage by 90, and a 1.46x increase in ROI for tool-use tasks.

Conclusion: E3-TIR effectively trains TIR agents with more efficiency and lower data cost, dynamically adapting knowledge boundaries and optimizing the training process.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR), existing training paradigms face significant limitations: Zero-RL suffers from inefficient exploration and mode degradation due to a lack of prior guidance, while SFT-then-RL is limited by high data costs and capability plateaus caused by low-entropy collapse. To address these challenges, we propose E3-TIR (Enhanced Experience Exploitation), a warm-up paradigm for the early stages of agent training. Specifically, we formulate training as the dynamic integration of three experience types: Expert Prefixes, Expert Guided, and Self-Exploration. By executing diverse branching exploration around expert "anchors" and employing a mix policy optimization mechanism, we effectively mitigate distribution shifts and resolve optimization conflicts arising from shared prefixes. Our method dynamically adapts the model's knowledge boundaries, effectively balancing exploration diversity with training efficiency.Experimental results demonstrate that E3-TIR achieves a 6 performance improvement over traditional paradigms on tool-use tasks, while requiring less than 10 of the synthetic data. Furthermore, in terms of ROI, a comprehensive metric integrating performance, data cost, and training efficiency we achieve a 1.46x gain compared to baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/yuki-younai/E3-TIR.

</details>


### [25] [Process Reward Agents for Steering Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09482)
*Jiwoong Sohn,Tomasz Sternal,Kenneth Styppa,Torsten Hoefler,Michael Moor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introducing Process Reward Agents (PRA), a method providing step-wise rewards during reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains, achieving state-of-the-art results on medical reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in reasoning within knowledge-intensive domains, where intermediate steps often require synthesis across large external sources and errors can propagate undetected.

Method: PRA dynamically integrates step-wise domain-grounded rewards during inference using a frozen policy, contrasting prior methods that only score completed trajectories post hoc.

Result: PRA achieves 80.8% accuracy on MedQA with Qwen3-4B and improves accuracy across unseen frozen policy models (0.5B to 8B parameters) by up to 25.7%, without requiring retraining.

Conclusion: PRA decouples frozen reasoners from domain-specific modules, enabling scalable and flexible deployment of reasoning systems in complex domains like medical reasoning.

Abstract: Reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains remains challenging as intermediate steps are often not locally verifiable: unlike math or code, evaluating step correctness may require synthesizing clues across large external knowledge sources. As a result, subtle errors can propagate through reasoning traces, potentially never to be detected. Prior work has proposed process reward models (PRMs), including retrieval-augmented variants, but these methods operate post hoc, scoring completed trajectories, which prevents their integration into dynamic inference procedures. Here, we introduce Process Reward Agents (PRA), a test-time method for providing domain-grounded, online, step-wise rewards to a frozen policy. In contrast to prior retrieval-augmented PRMs, PRA enables search-based decoding to rank and prune candidate trajectories at every generation step. Experiments on multiple medical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PRA consistently outperforms strong baselines, achieving 80.8% accuracy on MedQA with Qwen3-4B, a new state of the art at the 4B scale. Importantly, PRA generalizes to unseen frozen policy models ranging from 0.5B to 8B parameters, improving their accuracy by up to 25.7% without any policy model updates. More broadly, PRA suggests a paradigm in which frozen reasoners are decoupled from domain-specific reward modules, allowing the deployment of new backbones in complex domains without retraining.

</details>


### [26] [Strategic Algorithmic Monoculture:Experimental Evidence from Coordination Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09502)
*Gonzalo Ballestero,Hadi Hosseini,Samarth Khanna,Ran I. Shorrer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study explores how AI agents, particularly large language models (LLMs), adapt their behavior in multi-agent environments for effective coordination. It highlights LLMs' strong baseline similarity but limited ability to maintain diversity when incentivized.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the growing need for understanding coordination dynamics in multi-agent contexts, especially as AI agents become prominent in complex environments that demand cooperative actions.

Method: The researchers utilized an experimental framework involving both human participants and LLMs. They observed baseline action similarity and strategic adjustments in reaction to specific coordination incentives.

Result: LLMs displayed high baseline similarity comparable to humans, effectively coordinating on similar actions but struggling to sustain action divergence when such diversity was incentivized.

Conclusion: Large language models excel at coordination in situations favoring similarity but underperform when required to adapt and maintain heterogeneity, indicating areas for improvement in strategic behavioral diversity of AI systems.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly operate in multi-agent environments where outcomes depend on coordination. We distinguish primary algorithmic monoculture -- baseline action similarity -- from strategic algorithmic monoculture, whereby agents adjust similarity in response to incentives. We implement a simple experimental design that cleanly separates these forces, and deploy it on human and large language model (LLM) subjects. LLMs exhibit high levels of baseline similarity (primary monoculture) and, like humans, they regulate it in response to coordination incentives (strategic monoculture). While LLMs coordinate extremely well on similar actions, they lag behind humans in sustaining heterogeneity when divergence is rewarded.

</details>


### [27] [SUPERNOVA: Eliciting General Reasoning in LLMs with Reinforcement Learning on Natural Instructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08477)
*Ashima Suvarna,Kendrick Phan,Mehrab Beikzadeh,Hritik Bansal,Saadia Gabriel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The authors introduced SUPERNOVA, a data curation framework for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to improve general reasoning tasks for LLMs. SUPERNOVA leverages instruction-tuning datasets containing expert-annotated ground truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although RLVR has improved LLMs in formal reasoning domains (e.g., math, code), their performance on general reasoning tasks (e.g., causal inference and temporal understanding) remains limited due to a lack of diverse, high-quality, verifiable training data.

Method: The authors developed SUPERNOVA, which systematically adapts instruction-tuning datasets containing rich reasoning annotations for RLVR. They conducted over 100 controlled RL experiments analyzing the impact of data design choices, including source task selection, task mixing strategies, and synthetic data interventions.

Result: SUPERNOVA-trained models consistently outperformed strong baselines like Qwen3.5 on complex reasoning benchmarks (e.g., BBEH, Zebralogic, MMLU-Pro), with relative improvements of up to 52.8% on BBEH tasks across various model sizes.

Conclusion: SUPERNOVA demonstrates that principled data curation is critical for extending RLVR to general reasoning tasks. The findings provide actionable insights for designing high-quality human-annotated resources to enhance reasoning in LLMs. The code and data are publicly available.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly improved large language model (LLM) reasoning in formal domains such as mathematics and code. Despite these advancements, LLMs still struggle with general reasoning tasks requiring capabilities such as causal inference and temporal understanding. Extending RLVR to general reasoning is fundamentally constrained by the lack of high-quality, verifiable training data that spans diverse reasoning skills. To address this challenge, we propose SUPERNOVA, a data curation framework for RLVR aimed at enhancing general reasoning. Our key insight is that instruction-tuning datasets containing expert-annotated ground-truth encode rich reasoning patterns that can be systematically adapted for RLVR. To study this, we conduct 100+ controlled RL experiments to analyze how data design choices impact downstream reasoning performance. In particular, we investigate three key factors: (i) source task selection, (ii) task mixing strategies, and (iii) synthetic interventions for improving data quality. Our analysis reveals that source task selection is non-trivial and has a significant impact on downstream reasoning performance. Moreover, selecting tasks based on their performance for individual target tasks outperforms strategies based on overall average performance. Finally, models trained on SUPERNOVA outperform strong baselines (e.g., Qwen3.5) on challenging reasoning benchmarks including BBEH, Zebralogic, and MMLU-Pro. In particular, training on SUPERNOVA yields relative improvements of up to 52.8\% on BBEH across model sizes, demonstrating the effectiveness of principled data curation for RLVR. Our findings provide practical insights for curating human-annotated resources to extend RLVR to general reasoning. The code and data is available at https://github.com/asuvarna31/supernova.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [28] [Memory Wall is not gone: A Critical Outlook on Memory Architecture in Digital Neuromorphic Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08774)
*Amirreza Yousefzadeh,Sameed Sohail,Ana Lucia Varbanescu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper addresses a new memory bottleneck in digital neuromorphic processors caused by the on-chip memory such as SRAM and STT-MRAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine and address the emerging memory wall challenge in neuromorphic systems despite their goal of mitigating the memory bottleneck in conventional architectures.

Method: Critical analysis of energy and area efficiency in various on-chip memory technologies used in digital neuromorphic processors.

Result: On-chip memory, including SRAM and emerging technologies, consumes significant area and energy, creating a new memory bottleneck.

Conclusion: A reevaluation of memory organization is necessary to make digital neuromorphic processors viable for edge and embedded applications, and the paper discusses future research directions to address this.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of neuromorphic technology aims to address the memory wall challenge inherent in conventional von Neumann architectures. This paper critically examines current digital neuromorphic processors and their strategies to mitigate this bottleneck. While designed to bring computation closer to memory through distributed architectures, our findings indicate that on-chip memory systems, including SRAM and emerging technologies like STT-MRAM, have become significant consumers of area and energy, leading to a new memory wall. Through an analysis of energy and area efficiency in various memory technologies, we argue that without a re-evaluation of memory organization, digital neuromorphic processors may struggle to compete effectively in edge and embedded applications. We conclude with potential pathways for future research to overcome the limitations of on-chip memory in neuromorphic systems.

</details>


### [29] [From Indiscriminate to Targeted: Efficient RTL Verification via Functionally Key Signal-Driven LLM Assertion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08932)
*Yonghao Wang,Hongqin Lyu,Boling Chen,MinYang Bao,Wenchao Ding,Feng Gu,Zhiteng Chao,Jianan Mu,Kan Shi,Tiancheng Wang,Huawei Li*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AgileAssert, a framework for Assertion-Based Verification (ABV) that targets critical signals rather than indiscriminate verification. The approach reduces verification cost while maintaining or improving effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based methods in ABV focus on maximal coverage without prioritizing signal importance, leading to inefficiencies. The industry needs methods to maximize coverage with minimal cost by focusing on critical signals.

Method: The proposed AgileAssert framework constructs RTL semantic graphs, identifies top-K critical signals using a hybrid scoring-selection mechanism, applies structure-aware RTL slicing, and uses LLMs to generate targeted assertions.

Result: AgileAssert achieves a 66.68% reduction in assertions, improves coverage metrics, reduces token input consumption by 64%, and surpasses the error detection rate of existing methods while decreasing the average number of assertions by 72.74%.

Conclusion: AgileAssert shifts verification from indiscriminate to targeted, resulting in cost-efficient and effective functional verification. This approach outperforms existing SOTA methods substantially.

Abstract: Functional verification has become the most time-consuming phase in IC development, and Assertion-Based Verification (ABV) is key to reducing debugging time. However, existing LLM-based assertion generation methods typically pursue indiscriminate verification, aiming for maximal coverage without considering signal criticality, whereas industrial practice demands maximizing coverage with minimal verification cost. Consequently, identifying signals that have the greatest impact on design functionality and error propagation-enabling a shift from indiscriminate to targeted verification-remains a key challenge. To address this, we propose AgileAssert, a key signal-driven assertion generation framework that constructs RTL semantic graphs and identifies the top-K critical signals via a hybrid scoring and selection mechanism, followed by structure-aware RTL slicing to provide the LLM with precise targets and contextual information, thereby guiding LLMs to generate tightly constrained targeted assertions for efficient verification. Evaluated on block- and CPU-level designs, with an average 66.68% reduction in assertions, our approach outperforms three existing SOTA methods, and significantly improving coverage metrics while reducing input token consumption by 64%. In mutation testing, when our approach surpasses existing methods in error detection rate, the average number of assertions used decreases by 72.74%.

</details>


### [30] [DRIFT: Harnessing Inherent Fault Tolerance for Efficient and Reliable Diffusion Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.09073)
*Jinqi Wen,Tong Xie,Runsheng Wang,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces DRIFT, a framework to improve the efficiency and reliability of diffusion model inference using DVFS and fault-tolerance techniques, achieving significant energy savings and speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High energy consumption and latency in deploying diffusion models, despite their visual generation advantages; current methods fail to leverage their fault tolerance effectively.

Method: DRIFT combines resilience analysis, a resilience-aware DVFS strategy, ABFT rollback-based error correction, optimized offloading intervals, and reorganized data layouts.

Result: DRIFT achieves 36% energy savings via voltage underscaling or 1.7x speedup through overclocking without compromising output quality.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that utilizing fault tolerance properties in diffusion models can lead to substantial efficiency improvements while preserving generation quality.
